chunk_summary: This section provides brief, standardized counseling scripts for addressing common feeding problems in children, emphasizing practical, nonjudgmental advice tailored to specific issues like poor weight gain, milk overuse, and screen feeding.
learning_objectives: Understand how to counsel caregivers on improving dietary practices for children with poor weight gain or low solid intake.
Learn strategies to reduce reliance on packaged snacks, sugary drinks, and screen feeding during meals.
Apply practical, culturally sensitive demonstrations using household utensils to reinforce feeding advice.
Develop skills to set and track achievable dietary goals with caregivers.
key_facts: Diluted complementary foods can lead to poor weight gain; thicker foods with added oil or ghee are recommended.
After one year, solids should be prioritized over milk, with milk intake limited and spread across the day.
Daily packaged snacks and sugary drinks increase obesity and anemia risk; healthier snacks should be chosen and sugary drinks reserved for rare occasions.
Screen feeding disrupts hunger cues; meals should be screen-free, with one caregiver present, and limited to 20–30 minutes.
For low-literacy settings, demonstrations using the family’s own utensils and UNICEF IYCF counseling cards are effective.
reference_quote: For low-literacy settings, demonstrate changes using the family’s own katori and spoon and reinforce with UNICEF IYCF counseling cards.
document_key_topics: Reducing reliance on packaged snacks and sugary drinks
Promoting screen-free and structured mealtime habits
